story:
On a quiet weekday afternoon in a high-end department store, Madelyn hunted for a wedding gift from an old college friend’s registry. The porcelain serving bowl she chose felt safe—elegant, neutral—like the polite card and her mixed feelings about showing up. Then she saw him.

Mark. Beard, new gray at the temples, studying wine glasses like they were a puzzle. She ducked behind silver candleholders, then fled to the parking lot, heart shaking. Outside, she waited anyway, pretending to search her bag for keys she knew were in her pocket. When he walked out holding a small gold-wrapped box, he didn’t look surprised. “Hey, Mads,” he said—her old nickname, landing soft as the past.

They traded small words. He’d taken a year’s sabbatical in Spain, teaching in Barcelona. He’d come back for his homeland, his mother tongue, and his mother, who was sick. She told him their Lab, Bailey, had died. He remembered the dog with a tenderness that hurt. He suggested coffee. She reminded him she didn’t drink it. He grinned and recited her tea order—two bags, two sugars, a drop of honey—without a pause.

They walked arm in arm to a nearby coffee shop, the kind where a bell still rings on the door. Parallel talk helped. He was now department chair at Princeton; the lecherous old professor had finally retired. She still “dabbled” with painting, though too many canvases sat unfinished. His science fiction novel had joined the pile of abandoned dreams. They teased, they laughed, and then the quiet turned heavier.

“You look different,” she said. “Older. Settled.” He smiled, but not with his eyes.

“I was selfish. I hurt you. I never apologized,” he said. “I’m sorry.”

“I’m sorry too,” she answered. Therapy had taught her how to say it. It hadn’t taught her how to stop wondering about the what-ifs. They both circled the one day six years ago without saying their child’s name. The new house on Country Club Lane, the pool, the ninth hole. A golf ball arced into the water. Bailey, a water dog, did what he was born to do. A five-year-old followed, and everything after felt like the world folding in on itself. They’d chased a bigger life. The bill came due.

Mark’s hand shook; his tears came sudden and raw. He’d run because he couldn’t hold her pain and his own. She took his hands. An exhausted laugh cracked the grief for a breath. She watched his thumb circle the rim of his cup, an old tell for words he couldn’t say.

An hour later, they drifted back to safer ground. He walked her to her car, palm at the small of her back, a gesture that felt like time travel. The gift? Not for a girlfriend—he was single. A fraternity friend, Jack, was marrying Jenny, a woman from Madelyn’s art classes. Same wedding she was invited to. “Save me a dance?” he said, then clumsily spun her in a mock tango in the parking lot, both of them laughing.

Her hand rested on his shoulder. She felt the tremor there. She remembered the joy before the pool, before the guilt, before the silence. Maybe this unplanned meeting, made possible by someone else’s love, was the first step back toward light.
